## API Documentation

# Bob's Tips:
# 1. Use async/await for all I/O operations (file I/O, network calls)
# 2. Implement caching to avoid redundant API calls
# 3. Keep prompts in separate files for easy maintenance
# 4. Use type annotations for better code clarity
# 5. Handle errors gracefully and return structured error information
# 6. Use asyncio.gather() for concurrent execution when scanning multiple files
# 7. Consider adding a progress bar for long-running operations
# 8. Implement rate limiting if using external APIs
# 9. Add unit tests for each component
# 10. Document all functions with docstrings
```

## Quick Start Guide

# https://github.com/ashygee/bob
# Bob is a Python code documentation generator


# Minimal Setup Steps
# 1. Install dependencies
#    ```bash
#    pip install -r requirements.txt
#    ```
# 2. Create a WatsonX client instance
#    ```python
#    from lib.utils.watsonx import WatsonXClient
#    watsonx_client = WatsonXClient(api_key="your_api_key")
#    ```
# 3. Initialize QA Sentry
#    ```python
#    s = QASentry(watsonx_client)
#    ```

# Basic Usage Example
# ```python
# async def main():
#     # Initialize
#     s = QASentry(watsonx_client)
#     
#     # Scan a single file
#     result = await s.scan_code("example.py")
#     print(result)
#     
#     # Batch scan multiple files
#     results = await s.batch_scan(["file1.py", "file2.py"])
#     print(results)
#     
#     # Generate a report
#     report = s.generate_report(results)
#     print(report)
#     
# asyncio.run(main())
# ```

# Next Steps
# 1. Explore advanced configuration options in `prompts.py`
# 2. Customize test generation by modifying `test_persona.txt`
# 3. Integrate with your CI/CD pipeline using the batch scan functionality
# 4. Review the detailed documentation for each module:
#    - Agents: `lib/qa_sentry/agents.py`
#    - Chunking: `lib/qa_sentry/chunking.py`
#    - Auto Fixer: `lib/qa_sentry/auto_fixer.py`
#    - Git Utilities: `lib/qa_sentry/git_utils.py`
#    - Reporting: `lib/qa_sentry/reporting.py`

# Detailed Documentation
# - Agents: https://github.com/your-repo/qa-sentry/blob/main/lib/qa_sentry/agents.py
# - Chunking: https://github.com/your-repo/qa-sentry/blob/main/lib/qa_sentry/chunking.py
# - Auto Fixer: https://github.com/your-repo/qa-sentry/blob/main/lib/qa_sentry/auto_fixer.py
# - Git Utilities: https://github.com/your-repo/qa-sentry/blob/main/lib/qa_sentry/git_utils.py
# - Reporting: https://github.com/your-repo/qa-sentry/blob/main/lib/qa_sentry/reporting.py
# - Full Project: https://github.com/your-repo/qa-sentry

## User Manual

# https://www.bobheadxi.dev/introducing-bob/
# https://github.com/bobheadxi/bob
# https://github.com/bobheadxi/bob/tree/master/bob
# https://github.com/bobheadxi/bob/blob/master/bob/bob.py

# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #
#                       MAIN ENTRY POINT                               #
# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import json
    from lib.utils.watsonx_client import WatsonXClient

    # Initialize WatsonX client
    watsonx_client = WatsonXClient(
        api_key="YOUR_API_KEY",
        model="gpt-4o-mini"
    )

    # Initialize QA Sentry
    qasentry = QASentry(watsonx_client, enable_cache=True)

    # Example usage
    async def main():
        scan_result = await qasentry.scan_code(
            file_path="./example_code.py",
            scan_type="all",
            auto_fix=True
        )
        print(json.dumps(scan_result, indent=2))

        # Batch scan example
        batch_results = await qasentry.batch_scan(
            file_paths=["./example1.py", "./example2.py"],
            scan_type="quality",
            auto_fix=False
        )
        print(json.dumps(batch_results, indent=2))

        # Git diff scan example
        git_results = await qasentry.scan_git_diff(
            repo_path="./my_repo",
            staged=True,
            max_files=5,
            max_file_size_kb=1000
        )
        print(json.dumps(git_results, indent=2))

        # Test generation example
        test_result = await qasentry.generate_unit_tests(
            file_path="./example_code.py",
            testing_library="pytest",
            test_requirements="requirements.txt"
        )
        print(json.dumps(test_result, indent=2))

        # Report generation example
        report = qasentry.generate_report([scan_result])
        print(report)

    asyncio.run(main())

# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #
#                       CONFIGURATION OPTIONS                           #
# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #

# Environment Variables:
# - QASENTRY_CACHE: Enable/disable cache (default: true)
# - QASENTRY_CHUNK_SIZE: Override default chunk size (default: 1000)
# - QASENTRY_MAX_TOKENS: Override default max tokens (default: 4000)
# - QASENTRY_TEMPERATURE: Override default temperature (default: 0.1)

# Command Line Options:
# --cache: Enable/disable cache (overrides env var)
# --chunk-size: Set chunk size (overrides env var)
# --max-tokens: Set max tokens (overrides env var)
# --temperature: Set temperature (overrides env var)

# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #
#                       COMMON USE CASES                               #
# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #

# 1. Full production scan with auto-fix:
#   qasentry.scan_code("./app.py", scan_type="all", auto_fix=True)

# 2. Quality scan for multiple files with concurrent execution:
#   qasentry.batch_scan(["./service1.py", "./service2.py"], scan_type="quality")

# 3. Scan only changed files in git repo:
#   qasentry.scan_git_diff(repo_path="./my_project", staged=False)

# 4. Generate network performance tests for a service:
#   qasentry.generate_network_performance_tests("./service.py", testing_library="locust")

# 5. Generate unit tests with custom test requirements:
#   qasentry.generate_unit_tests(
#       file_path="./utils.py",
#       testing_library="pytest",
#       test_requirements="test_requirements.txt"
#   )

# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #
#                       BEST PRACTICES                                 #
# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #

# 1. Always use cache in production environments for performance gains.
#    Set QASENTRY_CACHE=true in your environment.

# 2. For large projects, use batch_scan for faster execution. It automatically
#    builds cross-file context when appropriate.

# 3. When using git scanning, set max_file_size_kb appropriately. For very large
#    files, consider increasing this limit or excluding them from scans.

# 4. For maximum security, run scans in a controlled environment with limited
#    permissions. The tool doesn't modify files unless explicitly told to do so
#    with the auto_fix parameter.

# 5. Regularly update your system prompt (reviewer.txt or system_context.md) to
#    keep up with the latest security vulnerabilities and coding standards.

# 6. When generating tests, provide clear requirements in test_requirements
#    files. The system uses this to generate more relevant tests.

# 7. For best results, analyze files after they've been committed but before
#    merging to main branches. This allows for early detection of issues.

# 8. When applying auto-fixes, review the changes carefully. While the tool
#    aims to make correct fixes, it's not infallible. Always test thoroughly.

# 9. For optimal performance, keep your WatsonX model up to date and choose
#    models appropriate for your needs (gpt-4o-mini for speed, gpt-4o for
#    higher accuracy).

# 10. Monitor your usage of WatsonX to avoid unexpected costs. The tool
#     provides detailed token usage in its output.

# 11. For complex projects, consider breaking down scans into smaller batches
#     to avoid hitting API rate limits.

# 12. Regularly review the generated reports and findings. The executive
#     summaries provide quick insights while the deep dives offer technical
#     details for remediation.

# 13. When integrating QA Sentry into CI/CD pipelines, consider using the
#     --cache option to avoid redundant scans of unchanged files.

# 14. For open-source projects, consider contributing your custom prompts and
#     test personas to the community for better collective security.

# 15. Always keep your QA Sentry installation updated. New versions may include
#     important security fixes and performance improvements.

## How-To Guide

# - 2024-08-29
# - 🎯 Objective: Implement a production-grade code quality analysis tool using multi-agent AI with debate pattern (Finder vs. Critic).
# - 🔧 Prerequisites:
#     - Python 3.9+ with asyncio support
#     - WatsonxClient instance configured for AI analysis
#     - Required libraries installed (from lib/ directory)
#     - reviewer.txt or prompts/system_context.md for system prompt
# - 🛠️ Step-by-Step Instructions:
#   1. Set up your environment:
#      - Ensure Python 3.9+ is installed
#      - Install required libraries (from lib/ directory)
#      - Configure WatsonxClient instance
#   2. Initialize QASentry:
#      ```python
#      from lib.qa_sentry.qa_sentry import QASentry
#      watsonx_client = WatsonxClient()  # Your configured client
#      analyzer = QASentry(watsonx_client)
#      ```
#   3. Scan a single file:
#      ```python
#      scan_results = await analyzer.scan_code("path/to/your/file.py", scan_type="all", auto_fix=False)
#      print(scan_results)
#      ```
#   4. Batch scan multiple files:
#      ```python
#      files = ["file1.py", "file2.py", "file3.py"]
#      batch_results = await analyzer.batch_scan(files, scan_type="all", auto_fix=False)
#      for result in batch_results:
#          print(result)
#      ```
#   5. Scan changed files from git diff:
#      ```python
#      git_results = await analyzer.scan_git_diff("/path/to/your/repo", staged=True, max_files=20)
#      print(git_results)
#      ```
#   6. Generate tests:
#      ```python
#      unit_tests = await analyzer.generate_unit_tests("path/to/your/file.py", testing_library="pytest")
#      print(unit_tests)
#      ```
#   7. Generate a report:
#      ```python
#      report = analyzer.generate_report(batch_results, output_path="qa_report.md")
#      print(report)
#      ```
# - ✅ Expected Outcomes:
#   - Comprehensive code analysis reports in JSON format
#   - Automatic test generation for unit, network, integration, and e2e tests
#   - Markdown reports summarizing findings
#   - Optional automatic code fixes applied
#   - Cached results for improved performance on unchanged files
# - ⚠️ Tips and Warnings:
#   - The chunk size is optimized at 1000 lines for reduced API calls
#   - File hash-based caching is enabled by default - disable if you need fresh analysis every time
#   - Auto-fix feature modifies the original files - use with caution and version control
#   - For large projects, consider using batch_scan with additional context for cross-file analysis
#   - The system prompt is loaded from reviewer.txt or prompts/system_context.md - customize for your needs
#   - The temperature is set to 0.1 for strict technical mode - increase for more creative suggestions
#   - Monitor API usage as the system makes multiple calls per analysis
#   - For very large files, ensure your environment has sufficient memory to handle the chunking process
#   - When applying auto-fixes, always review the changes before committing
#   - The tool is designed for Python files but can analyze other languages with proper configuration
#   - For optimal performance, run batch scans concurrently with asyncio.gather()
#   - Regularly update the library dependencies for improved analysis models and features
# - 📦 How to Run:
#   1. Clone the repository containing this code
#   2. Install dependencies: `pip install -r requirements.txt`
#   3. Configure your WatsonxClient
#   4. Run the script with `python your_script.py`
# - 📚 Additional Resources:
#   - WatsonxClient documentation for AI analysis configuration
#   - lib/utils/cache.py for cache management details
#   - lib/qa_sentry/reporting.py for report customization options
#   - lib/qa_sentry/auto_fixer.py for understanding auto-fix capabilities
#   - lib/qa_sentry/test_generator.py for test generation customization
# - 📌 Maintenance Notes:
#   - Regularly update prompts/system_context.md for improved analysis quality
#   - Monitor and adjust the temperature setting based on desired creativity vs. precision
#   - Periodically review and update the system_prompt loading mechanism
#   - Consider implementing a logging system for production environments
#   - For enterprise use, implement authentication for the QASentry class
# - 📂 Directory Structure:
#   ```
#   /qa_sentry
#   ├── __init__.py
#   ├── agents.py
#   ├── chunking.py
#   ├── test_generator.py
#   ├── reporting.py
#   ├── prompts.py
#   ├── parsers.py
#   ├── auto_fixer.py
#   ├── git_utils.py
#   └── qa_sentry.py (this file)
#   ```

## Tutorial

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
#                           LEARNING OBJECTIVES                          #
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
"""
1. Understand the architecture of a multi-agent AI code scanner
2. Learn how to orchestrate complex workflows with asyncio
3. Master large file chunking techniques for efficient API usage
4. Implement caching strategies for performance optimization
5. Create production-grade code analysis pipelines
6. Generate dynamic tests for different testing types
7. Produce structured reports from analysis results
"""

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
#                            PREREQUISITES                              #
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
"""
- Basic Python programming skills
- Familiarity with asynchronous programming (async/await)
- Understanding of JSON data structures
- Knowledge of Git basics (diff, staging)
- Experience with AI APIs (Watsonx used in this example)
- Familiarity with code quality analysis concepts
"""

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
#                            STEP-BY-STEP LESSONS                       #
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
"""
Step 1: Understanding the Architecture
- Examine the QASentry class and its responsibilities
- Identify the main components: agents, chunking, test_generator, reporting
- Understand the debate pattern (Finder vs Critic)

Step 2: Initializing the Scanner
- Learn how to create a QASentry instance
- Explore initialization parameters (client, caching)
- Understand system prompts and their importance

Step 3: Scanning Individual Files
- Analyze the scan_code method signature and parameters
- Understand the file reading process and error handling
- Learn about language detection and its role
- Explore chunking logic for large files
- Understand caching mechanism and when it's applied

Step 4: Implementing the Debate Pattern
- Study the finder_pass and critic_pass functions
- Understand how findings are passed between agents
- Learn about the two-pass analysis process

Step 5: Caching Mechanism
- Examine the cache implementation in lib.utils.cache
- Understand how cache keys are generated
- Learn about cache hit/miss logic

Step 6: Auto-Fix Feature
- Explore the apply_auto_fixes function
- Understand how fixes are applied to code
- Learn about the integration with scan_code

Step 7: Git Diff Scanning
- Study the scan_git_diff method
- Understand how git diff is processed
- Learn about configurable limits for scanning

Step 8: Batch Scanning
- Analyze the batch_scan method
- Understand concurrent execution with asyncio.gather
- Learn about cross-file context building

Step 9: Test Generation
- Explore generate_network_performance_tests and generate_unit_tests
- Understand how different test types are generated
- Learn about test_requirements parameter

Step 10: Reporting
- Study the generate_report method
- Understand markdown report structure
- Learn about output options
"""

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
#                              PRACTICE EXERCISES                       #
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
"""
Exercise 1: Customizing System Prompt
- Modify the _load_system_context method to load a custom prompt from a file
- Create a new prompt file with specific instructions for your use case
- Test how the custom prompt affects the analysis results

Exercise 2: Implementing a New Test Type
- Add a new test_type (e.g., "security") to generate_security_tests method
- Implement the necessary logic in lib.qa_sentry.test_generator
- Modify the generate_tests method to handle the new test type
- Test your implementation with a sample code file

Exercise 3: Optimizing Cache Usage
- Analyze the current caching mechanism in scan_code
- Implement a more sophisticated cache key generation strategy
- Test how your optimization affects cache hit rates
- Measure performance improvements

Exercise 4: Adding a New Agent
- Create a new agent called "reviewer" that performs a third pass analysis
- Implement the reviewer_pass function in lib.qa_sentry.agents
- Integrate this new agent into the scan_code workflow
- Test how the additional analysis affects the results

Exercise 5: Implementing Batch Auto-Fix
- Modify the batch_scan method to apply auto-fixes to all files
- Implement error handling for auto-fix application
- Test your implementation with a set of files containing known issues
"""

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
#                            SUMMARY AND NEXT STEPS                     #
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------- #
"""
Summary:
You've learned how to build and use a comprehensive code quality analysis system with multi-agent AI scanning. You've explored:

1. The overall architecture of a multi-agent code scanner
2. How to orchestrate complex workflows with asyncio
3. Efficient techniques for handling large files
4. Performance optimization through caching
5. Production-grade code analysis pipelines
6. Dynamic test generation for different testing types
7. Structured reporting of analysis results

Next Steps:
1. Experiment with different AI models and observe how they affect the analysis
2. Integrate this system with your CI/CD pipeline for continuous code quality checks
3. Create custom test personas in test_persona.txt for specific project needs
4. Explore more advanced AI prompting techniques for better analysis
5. Contribute to the open-source community by adding new features or optimizations
6. Study the documentation of each component to understand their full capabilities
7. Implement a dashboard to visualize the analysis results over time
"""

## Troubleshooting Guide

# (https://github.com/bobcodeai/qasentry)

# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #
#                         TROUBLESHOOTING GUIDE                       #
# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #

class TroubleshootingGuide:
    """Comprehensive troubleshooting guide for QA Sentry Orchestrator"""

    @staticmethod
    def common_errors_and_solutions():
        """
        Common issues encountered during usage and their solutions.
        """
        return {
            "FileNotFoundError": {
                "description": "File path is incorrect or file doesn't exist",
                "solution": "Verify file_path is correct and the file exists at that location"
            },
            "PermissionError": {
                "description": "Insufficient permissions to read the file",
                "solution": "Ensure the process has read permissions for the file"
            },
            "WatsonXClientError": {
                "description": "Error communicating with WatsonX AI service",
                "solution": "Check WatsonX service status, API keys, and network connectivity"
            },
            "AsyncioTimeoutError": {
                "description": "Timeout during AI analysis",
                "solution": "Increase max_tokens or reduce chunk_size in configuration"
            },
            "JSONDecodeError": {
                "description": "Invalid JSON output from AI model",
                "solution": "Check system_prompt formatting and ensure valid JSON schema"
            },
            "RecursionError": {
                "description": "Excessive recursion during deep analysis",
                "solution": "Reduce chunk_size or simplify complex code structures"
            },
            "CacheKeyCollision": {
                "description": "Cache key collisions causing stale results",
                "solution": "Ensure unique file paths and scan types in cache keys"
            }
        }

    @staticmethod
    def debugging_steps():
        """
        Step-by-step debugging guide for developers working on QA Sentry.
        """
        return [
            "1. Enable DEBUG logging: Set log_level='DEBUG' in your configuration",
            "2. Use the --debug flag when running the CLI for verbose output",
            "3. Isolate the issue by running with a single file (--single-file path/to/file)",
            "4. Check WatsonX service status if you encounter API errors",
            "5. Verify your system_prompt format using the validate_prompt utility",
            "6. For cache issues, run with --no-cache to bypass cached results",
            "7. If finding false positives, adjust temperature parameter (0.0-0.2 recommended)",
            "8. For performance issues, profile with: python -m cProfile -o profile.out your_script.py",
            "9. Use the built-in profiler: python -m qa_sentry.profile path/to/file",
            "10. Report bugs with full traceback and configuration details to our issue tracker"
        ]

    @staticmethod
    def faq():
        """
        Frequently asked questions about QA Sentry usage and configuration.
        """
        return {
            "How do I configure the chunk size?": {
                "answer": "Chunk size is set via the chunk_size attribute in the QASentry class. "
                         "Default is 1000 lines. Adjust based on your file sizes and AI token limits."
            },
            "Can I disable caching?": {
                "answer": "Yes, set enable_cache=False when initializing QASentry to disable caching"
            },
            "What file types are supported?": {
                "answer": "QA Sentry automatically detects over 100 programming languages. "
                         "For non-code files, set language='text' manually."
            },
            "How do I customize the system prompt?": {
                "answer": "Modify the reviewer.txt file or system_context.md in the prompts/ directory. "
                         "The system prompt is loaded in _load_system_context method."
            },
            "I'm getting 'Rate limit exceeded' errors from WatsonX. What should I do?": {
                "answer": "Implement exponential backoff retry logic or contact WatsonX support for higher limits"
            },
            "How can I analyze multiple files concurrently?": {
                "answer": "Use the batch_scan method which utilizes asyncio.gather for concurrent execution"
            },
            "What's the difference between finder and critic passes?": {
                "answer": "Finder pass identifies potential issues, while critic pass validates and expands on them. "
                         "This debate pattern improves accuracy."
            },
            "Can I integrate QA Sentry with CI/CD pipelines?": {
                "answer": "Yes, QA Sentry is designed for CI/CD integration. Use the scan_code or batch_scan methods "
                         "in your pipeline scripts to analyze code on each commit."
            }
        }

    @staticmethod
    def known_issues():
        """
        Documented known issues and their workarounds.
        """
        return {
            "False positives in pattern matching": {
                "description": "Occasionally, the AI might flag valid patterns as issues",
                "workaround": "Adjust temperature to lower values (0.0-0.1) or add specific exceptions in prompts"
            },
            "Performance degradation with very large files (>10,000 lines)": {
                "description": "Chunking may cause context loss for extremely large files",
                "workaround": "Split files manually or implement custom chunking logic for specific file types"
            },
            "Cache not updating after code changes": {
                "description": "Hash-based cache might not detect certain changes",
                "workaround": "Use --no-cache flag or manually clear cache with cache.clear()"
            },
            "Compatibility issues with Python 3.6": {
                "description": "Some features require Python 3.7+",
                "workaround": "Upgrade to Python 3.7 or later"
            },
            "AI service downtime causing pipeline failures": {
                "description": "External AI service unavailability impacts analysis",
                "workaround": "Implement retry logic with exponential backoff or use local model fallback"
            }
        }

    @staticmethod
    def support_resources():
        """
        Resources for getting help and contributing to QA Sentry.
        """
        return {
            "GitHub Repository": "https://github.com/bobcodeai/qasentry",
            "Issue Tracker": "https://github.com/bobcodeai/qasentry/issues",
            "Documentation": "https://qasentry.readthedocs.io",
            "Community Forum": "https://forum.qasentry.ai",
            "Support Email": "support@qasentry.ai",
            "Slack Community": "https://slack.qasentry.ai",
            "Contribution Guidelines": "https://github.com/bobcodeai/qasentry/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md",
            "Code of Conduct": "https://github.com/bobcodeai/qasentry/blob/main/CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md"
        }

# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #
#                         END OF TROUBLESHOOTING                       #
# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #

# Example usage:
# async def main():
#     watsonx_client = WatsonXClient(api_key="your_api_key")
#     qasentry = QASentry(watsonx_client, enable_cache=True)
#     results = await qasentry.scan_code("path/to/your/file.py", scan_type="all", auto_fix=True)
#     print(results)
#
# asyncio.run(main())
```

## Requirements Specification

# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #
#                       SYSTEM CONSTRAINTS                             #
# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #

# 1. Performance Requirements:
#    - Must process files >1000 lines within 30 seconds on a standard cloud VM (4 vCPU, 16GB RAM)
#    - Concurrent batch scanning should achieve at least 2x speedup compared to sequential processing
#    - Cache hit rate should be >= 80% for repeated scans of unchanged files

# 2. Security Constraints:
#    - Must not execute code or evaluate expressions from the scanned files
#    - All external API calls (WatsonxClient) must be made through secure channels
#    - Sensitive information in scan results must be redacted in reports when requested

# 3. Compatibility Requirements:
#    - Must support Python files from version 3.7 to 3.12
#    - Must work with both Unix and Windows line endings
#    - Must handle UTF-8, UTF-16, and ASCII encoded files

# 4. Scalability Constraints:
#    - The batch_scan function must handle up to 1000 files concurrently without memory overflow
#    - System must gracefully degrade performance when Watsonx API rate limits are hit

# 5. Maintenance Requirements:
#    - All prompts must be centralized in prompts.py for easy maintenance
#    - System prompt must be easily updatable without code changes
#    - Code must adhere to PEP 8 standards with a maximum line length of 120 characters

# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #
#                       NON-FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS                   #
# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #

# 1. Reliability:
#    - Must handle file read errors gracefully and report them without crashing
#    - Must implement retry logic for transient Watsonx API errors (max 3 attempts with exponential backoff)
#    - Must provide comprehensive error reporting including traceback information

# 2. Usability:
#    - Must provide clear error messages for common issues (e.g., unsupported file types)
#    - Must allow configuration of chunk size, temperature, and max tokens through constructor
#    - Must support optional auto-fix feature with safety checks before applying changes

# 3. Maintainability:
#    - Must have clear separation of concerns with dedicated modules for each functionality
#    - Must include type annotations for all public methods and complex data structures
#    - Must include comprehensive docstrings for all public methods

# 4. Testability:
#    - Must have unit tests covering all public methods
#    - Must mock external dependencies (WatsonxClient) in tests
#    - Must include integration tests for the full scan pipeline

# 5. Extensibility:
#    - Must allow easy addition of new scan types through the scan_type parameter
#    - Must support custom system prompts through the constructor
#    - Must allow configuration of analysis temperature and token limits

# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #
#                       DEPENDENCIES                                   #
# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #

# 1. Python Standard Library:
#    - asyncio
#    - pathlib
#    - typing
#    - traceback

# 2. Third-Party Libraries:
#    - WatsonxClient (external AI client)
#    - (Assumed) lib.utils, lib.qa_sentry.*, and other internal modules

# 3. System Dependencies:
#    - Python 3.7 or higher
#    - Access to Watsonx API with valid credentials
#    - Sufficient disk space for caching (default 10GB)

# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #
#                       ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA                            #
# ------------------------------------------------------------------ #

# 1. Functional Acceptance:
#    - The system must produce JSON reports with the specified schema
#    - The system must correctly identify and report at least 90% of known vulnerabilities in test codebases
#    - The auto-fix feature must correctly apply at least 80% of suggested fixes without introducing new errors

# 2. Performance Acceptance:
#    - The system must process a 5000-line Python file in under 20 seconds
#    - The batch_scan function must process 100 files in under 2 minutes on a standard cloud VM
#    - The cache system must reduce processing time by at least 50% for repeated scans of unchanged files

# 3. Quality Acceptance:
#    - The code must achieve 90%+ coverage in unit tests
#    - The code must pass pylint with a minimum score of 9/10
#    - The system must generate reports that are human-readable and actionable

# 4. Security Acceptance:
#    - The system must not expose any sensitive information from the scanned code in reports without explicit request
#    - The system must validate all inputs to prevent injection attacks

# 5. Compatibility Acceptance:
#    - The system must successfully scan sample codebases written for Python versions 3.7 to 3.12
#    - The system must correctly handle files with mixed line endings
#    - The system must correctly process files encoded in UTF-8, UTF-16, and ASCII

# 6. Scalability Acceptance:
#    - The system must handle a batch scan of 1000 Python files without crashing
#    - The system must gracefully handle and report API rate limit errors from Watsonx

# 7. Maintainability Acceptance:
#    - The system prompt must be updatable without modifying core code
#    - All prompts must be located in a single location (prompts.py)
#    - The code must adhere to PEP 8 standards with a maximum line length of 120 characters

# 8. Extensibility Acceptance:
#    - A new scan type 'security_audit' can be added by implementing a new method in agents.py and updating the scan_type parameter
#    - The system prompt can be customized by passing a different string to the constructor
#    - The analysis temperature can be adjusted by changing the constructor parameter
